title_en: The Ansar: Companions of Medina and Their Virtues
title_ar: الأنصار وفضائلهم وذكر قبيلتي الأوس والخزرج
content_en:
The Ansar — the 'Helpers' of Medina who received, sheltered, and supported the Prophet and the Muhajirun — occupy a place in Islamic history second only to the Muhajirun themselves in terms of honor and distinction. They belonged principally to two Medinan tribes: the Aws and the Khazraj, both of Arab origin who had settled in the agricultural oasis of Yathrib (which became Medina, the City of the Prophet, after the Hijra). Before Islam, the Aws and Khazraj were in a state of chronic warfare with each other; the coming of Islam transformed them from bitter enemies into brothers in faith, a transformation celebrated throughout the Quran.

Ibn 'Abd al-Barr's entries on the Ansar constitute a significant portion of Al-Isti'ab. He documents the two pledges of 'Aqaba — the preliminary covenants made between the Prophet and representatives of the Medinan tribes before the Hijra — as the founding moments of the Ansar identity. The First Pledge of 'Aqaba (11th year of prophethood) involved twelve Medinan men who pledged to worship Allah alone and to abstain from major sins. The Second Pledge of 'Aqaba (12th year) involved seventy-three men and two women who pledged to protect the Prophet as they would protect their own families and children, effectively inviting him and the Muslim community to migrate to Medina.

Among the most celebrated of the Ansar is Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (d. 5 AH / 627 CE), the chief of the Aws tribe and one of the most significant figures in the early Medinan community. His entry in Al-Isti'ab records his conversion to Islam — brought about largely through the efforts of Mus'ab ibn 'Umayr, the Prophet's emissary to Medina — and his subsequent role as the leader of his tribe in accepting Islam. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's most historically significant act was his arbitration of the fate of the Banu Qurayza following the Battle of the Trench. His judgment — that the men among them be executed and the women and children be enslaved, for their treachery during the siege — was accepted by the Prophet and executed. Sa'd died shortly after from wounds sustained during the battle. The Prophet said that the Throne of Allah shook with joy at the arrival of Sa'd's soul.

Sa'd ibn 'Ubada (d. 15 AH / 636 CE), the chief of the Khazraj and one of the Ansar leaders nominated for the caliphate after the Prophet's death, is treated in Al-Isti'ab with the complexity his historical role demands. He was a man of great generosity and courage, known for his hospitality and his service to the early community. His non-submission to Abu Bakr's caliphate and his eventual death in Syria are recorded by Ibn 'Abd al-Barr without partisan judgment — reflecting the Al-Isti'ab's general approach of documenting the Companions' histories fairly while affirming the rightness of Abu Bakr's caliphate according to the consensus of Ahl al-Sunnah.

The female Companions among the Ansar are also treated with care in Al-Isti'ab. Nusayba bint Ka'b (Umm 'Amara), who fought to defend the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud when many warriors had fled, is one of the most celebrated female warriors in Islamic history. Her entry records that she sustained multiple wounds while fighting hand to hand against the enemies of the Prophet — a display of courage that the Prophet himself acknowledged with praise. The Ansar women generally had a higher degree of religious knowledge and participation in public life than was common among women in the ancient world, a feature of early Islamic society that Al-Isti'ab's biographical entries help illuminate.

Ibn 'Abd al-Barr's treatment of the Ansar also addresses their relationship with the Muhajirun and the institution of mu'akhah (brotherhood) that the Prophet established between members of the two groups. This pairing — in which each Muhajirun who had left his property in Mecca was paired with an Ansar who shared his home and wealth — was an act of unprecedented social solidarity that provided the material foundation for the early community. The hadith 'The Ansar are my ear-ring and my trusted ones' and 'Were it not for the Hijra, I would have been one of the Ansar' express the Prophet's profound love for the Helpers who made the Islamic community in Medina possible.
content_ar: الأنصار هم أهل المدينة الذين آووا النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونصروه، وهم من قبيلتي الأوس والخزرج.
title_id: Kaum Ansar: Para Sahabat Madinah dan Keutamaan Mereka
content_id:
Kaum Ansar — para 'Penolong' Madinah yang menerima, menampung, dan mendukung Nabi ﷺ serta kaum Muhajirin — menduduki tempat dalam sejarah Islam yang kedua hanya setelah kaum Muhajirin sendiri dalam hal kehormatan dan kemuliaan. Mereka terutama berasal dari dua suku Madinah: Aus dan Khazraj, keduanya berketurunan Arab yang telah menetap di oasis pertanian Yatsrib (yang kemudian menjadi Madinah, Kota Nabi, setelah Hijra). Sebelum Islam, Aus dan Khazraj berada dalam keadaan perang kronis satu sama lain; datangnya Islam mengubah mereka dari musuh bebuyutan menjadi saudara seiman, sebuah transformasi yang dirayakan di seluruh Al-Qur'an.

Entri-entri Ibn 'Abd al-Barr tentang kaum Ansar merupakan bagian yang signifikan dari Al-Isti'ab. Beliau mendokumentasikan dua baiat 'Aqabah — perjanjian-perjanjian awal yang dibuat antara Nabi ﷺ dan perwakilan-perwakilan suku-suku Madinah sebelum Hijra — sebagai momen-momen pendirian identitas Ansar. Baiat 'Aqabah Pertama (tahun ke-11 kenabian) melibatkan dua belas laki-laki Madinah yang berjanji untuk menyembah Allah semata dan menjauhkan diri dari dosa-dosa besar. Baiat 'Aqabah Kedua (tahun ke-12) melibatkan tujuh puluh tiga laki-laki dan dua perempuan yang berjanji untuk melindungi Nabi ﷺ sebagaimana mereka melindungi keluarga dan anak-anak mereka sendiri, secara efektif mengundang beliau dan komunitas Muslim untuk berhijrah ke Madinah.

Di antara yang paling terkenal dari kaum Ansar adalah Sa'd ibn Mu'adz (wafat 5 H / 627 M), kepala suku Aus dan salah satu figur paling signifikan dalam komunitas Madinah awal. Entri beliau dalam Al-Isti'ab mencatat pemelukan Islamnya — yang sebagian besar dilakukan melalui upaya Mush'ab ibn 'Umayr, utusan Nabi ﷺ ke Madinah — dan perannya selanjutnya sebagai pemimpin sukunya dalam menerima Islam. Tindakan Sa'd ibn Mu'adz yang paling signifikan secara historis adalah arbitrasinya atas nasib Banu Quraizhah setelah Pertempuran Khandaq. Putusannya — bahwa para laki-laki di antara mereka dihukum mati dan perempuan serta anak-anak dijadikan tawanan, atas pengkhianatan mereka selama pengepungan — dikonfirmasi oleh Nabi ﷺ sebagai sesuai dengan keputusan Allah.

Entri Al-Isti'ab tentang kaum Ansar dengan meyakinkan menggambarkan pentingnya mereka yang tidak dapat digantikan dalam memungkinkan perkembangan komunitas Muslim. Tanpa penerimaan dan perlindungan mereka, umat Islam tidak akan memiliki tempat yang aman untuk membangun komunitas mereka dan mengembangkan institusi-institusi yang pada akhirnya mengubah sejarah. Dokumen biografi yang kaya yang dilestarikan oleh Ibn 'Abd al-Barr memungkinkan kita untuk memahami Ansar bukan sebagai kelompok yang tidak dibedakan melainkan sebagai individu-individu yang, dengan pilihan-pilihan dan pengorbanan-pengorbanan spesifik mereka, membuktikan iman yang memungkinkan risalah Islam bertahan dan berkembang.
